title: Working with the part that hoards as a form of safety
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
The pile that keeps you safe 
 I have a closet that I don't open. It's not just messy. It's a geological formation. Layers of things I might need someday....
htmlContent:
The pile that keeps you safe

I have a closet that I don't open. It's not just messy. It's a geological formation. Layers of things I might need someday. Clothes that don't fit. Boxes I can't remember packing. And I know I'm not alone in this. You probably have a space like that too. A drawer. A room. A digital folder with thousands of unread emails. A pantry stocked like the apocalypse is coming tomorrow.

Here's the thing about hoarding. We usually think of it as a problem of too much stuff. A lack of discipline. A failure to organize. But that's not what it is. Not really. The part that hoards isn't lazy or out of control. It's terrified. It's trying to keep you safe in the only way it knows how.

Think about it. When you were small and the world felt unpredictable, what could you control? Not your parents. Not the chaos. Not the pain. But you could hold onto something. A stuffed animal. A collection of rocks. A stack of magazines. That thing became an anchor. A promise that not everything would disappear. Stay with me here. That part of you is still operating. It's still scanning for scarcity. Still preparing for the worst. Still holding onto everything because letting go feels like falling into an abyss.

I've worked with this part in myself for years. And I've seen it in so many clients. It's not a flaw. It's a survival strategy. And we have to treat it with the same respect we'd give any protector part. Because it's been carrying a heavy load for a very long time.

The nervous system behind the clutter

Here's what researchers like Stephen Porges and Deb Dana have shown us. The nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When your body detects threat, it shifts into protective states. Fight. Flight. Freeze. And sometimes, a lesser-known response called "appease" or "submit." But there's another one that doesn't get talked about enough. The "gather" response.

Think of animals hoarding food before winter. That's a survival instinct. Your nervous system doesn't know the difference between a real famine and an emotional one. When you've experienced deprivation, neglect, or unpredictable caregiving, your body learns one thing: there's never enough. Never enough love. Never enough safety. Never enough resources. So it starts stockpiling. Not just food or money. But objects. Information. Relationships. Anything that feels like it might run out.

I remember a client who couldn't throw away a single receipt. She had shoeboxes full of them going back years. When I asked her what would happen if she tossed them, she said, "I'd lose proof that I existed." Wild, right? But it makes sense. When your history has been erased, minimized, or denied, the physical evidence becomes your only witness. The hoarding part isn't collecting stuff. It's collecting evidence of your life. Your worth. Your right to take up space.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma gets stored in the body. It's not just a memory in your head. It's a felt sense in your tissues. And the hoarding part is often holding onto objects because they carry a somatic charge. Letting go of that old sweater isn't just releasing fabric. It's releasing the grief of who you were when you wore it. The loss of that time. The death of that version of you. That's heavy. That's real.

Meeting the hoarding part with curiosity, not contempt

So how do we work with this part? Not by forcing it to change. Not by throwing everything away in a purge that leaves you shaking and dissociated. That's retraumatizing. That's the wellness industry's approach. Just declutter your life. Just Marie Kondo everything that doesn't spark joy. But what if nothing sparks joy? What if your nervous system is so frozen that you can't feel anything at all? Then what?

We start with curiosity. Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems, calls this "unblending." You step back from the part. You don't become it. You don't fight it. You just notice it. You say, "Hello. I see you there. I know you're trying to protect me." And you listen. Not with judgment. With genuine interest.

Ask the part: What are you afraid would happen if we let this go? What are you protecting me from? What do you need me to know?

The answers might surprise you. Maybe it's not about the stuff at all. Maybe the hoarding part is holding onto shame. Maybe it's trying to create a physical barrier between you and the world. Maybe it's the only way you know how to say "no" when everyone around you demanded "yes." Maybe it's a way of keeping yourself so busy and overwhelmed that you don't have to feel the deeper pain underneath.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker does an incredible job of naming the inner critic and the parts that keep us stuck in survival mode. If you haven't read it, I'd recommend it. It helps you understand why your system is doing what it's doing.

The slow work of releasing

Let me be honest with you. This work is not fast. It's not a weekend project. It's not about buying matching storage bins and feeling better. The hoarding part has been on duty for decades. It's not going to trust you overnight. And it shouldn't. Trust has to be earned. Even from yourself.

I started by making small agreements with the part. "I'm not going to throw anything away today. I'm just going to open the closet door and look at it for five minutes." That's it. Just looking. The first time I did this, my heart was pounding. My hands were shaking. I felt like I was going to die. But I stayed. I breathed. I told the part, "I'm not leaving you. I'm right here."

Over time, I started asking permission. "Can I move this box from the floor to the shelf? We're not getting rid of it. Just moving it." Sometimes the part said no. And I listened. That was huge. That was the first time in my life I had respected a boundary set by my own system. Not pushed through. Not ignored. Just honored it.

Eventually, we started letting go of things. One at a time. A broken mug. A shirt with a stain. A book I'd never read. Each release was a negotiation. A conversation. A small funeral for the fear that letting go would destroy me.

For understanding how trauma fragments the self, a popular choice is Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link). Janina Fisher's work is brilliant for mapping out these parts and learning how to relate to them with compassion instead of control. It's not an easy read, but it's worth the effort.

Safety as an inside job

Here's what I've come to understand. The hoarding part isn't actually trying to protect you from losing stuff. It's trying to protect you from feeling the loss that's already happened. The grief that never got processed. The abandonment that never got held. The terror of being small and helpless in a world that didn't care.

When you start to build real safety in your nervous system, the need to hoard begins to soften. Not disappear. But soften. You start to trust that you can survive without the pile. That you are enough, even when you have nothing. That your worth isn't tied to what you own or how much you've accumulated.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work is like a warm blanket for the parts that feel unworthy. She teaches you to hold your experience with kindness, even when it's messy and uncomfortable. Especially then.

I'm not going to tell you that you'll ever be perfectly organized. Or that the hoarding part will fully retire. Honestly, I don't think it should. It's a part of you. It has wisdom. It knows how to survive. And that's valuable. But maybe it can take a lighter role. Maybe it can rest more. Maybe it can trust that you're an adult now, and you have other resources. Other ways to cope. Other parts that can help.

Sit with that. What would it feel like to not have to hold everything so tightly? What would it feel like to let the part know that you're here now, and you're not going anywhere? That you can handle the grief. That you can hold the pain. That you can survive the emptiness.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did something shift? Did you tense up? Did you feel a wave of relief or fear? That's the part responding. It's listening. It's watching to see if you really mean it.

This work is a relationship. Not a project. And like any relationship, it takes time, patience, and a lot of showing up. Even when it's hard. Even when you'd rather just buy another storage bin and pretend everything is fine.

But you're here. Reading this. That means some part of you is ready. Ready to look. Ready to listen. Ready to start the slow, tender work of coming home to yourself.

That's enough. That's more than enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Healing the Fragmented Selves of Trauma Survivors (paid link) - For understanding how trauma fragments the self
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - A resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
